name: OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications (2025)
description: The OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications highlights the most critical security risks associated with deploying and managing LLMs. The 2025 edition reflects the rapidly evolving threat landscape for AI-powered applications, covering risks from prompt injection and sensitive information disclosure to supply chain vulnerabilities and unbounded resource consumption. This guide helps organizations understand and mitigate the unique security challenges posed by LLM integrations.

controls.title: Prompt Injection
controls.description: Prompt Injection occurs when an attacker manipulates a large language model through crafted inputs that cause the model to bypass its intended instructions or security controls. This vulnerability arises when untrusted data is incorporated into prompts without proper validation or sanitization, allowing adversaries to override system instructions. Successful exploitation can lead to unauthorized data access, social engineering, or the execution of unintended actions through the LLM's connected systems.
controls.discussion: Organizations should implement strict input validation and sanitization of all user inputs before they reach the LLM, filtering for suspicious patterns and potentially malicious instructions. System prompts should be designed with clear delimiters to distinguish between system instructions and user-provided content. Access controls should limit LLM permissions to only necessary functions following the principle of least privilege. Additionally, output validation and detailed logging of all LLM interactions are essential for detecting and responding to injection attempts.

controls.title: Sensitive Information Disclosure
controls.description: Sensitive Information Disclosure occurs when a large language model inadvertently exposes confidential data through its outputs. The risk stems from models potentially leaking sensitive information encountered during training, embedded in system prompts, or provided through user interactions. This could include personal data, credentials, proprietary business information, or other protected content, and may occur through direct responses, training data memorization, or side-channel inference.
controls.discussion: Implement robust data sanitization to prevent sensitive information from being included in training datasets or system prompt contexts. Deploy output monitoring and detection systems to identify and redact sensitive data patterns such as PII, credentials, and proprietary information before responses reach users. Restrict model access to sensitive information through proper access controls and the principle of least privilege. Consider techniques like differential privacy during model training and maintain comprehensive audit logs of model interactions to detect and investigate potential data leakage incidents.

controls.title: Supply Chain
controls.description: Supply chain vulnerabilities in LLM systems arise when third-party components, training data sources, pre-trained models, or plugins contain weaknesses that could be exploited. These vulnerabilities can be introduced through compromised dependencies, malicious training data, or insecure model repositories. The attack surface extends across the entire lifecycle of model development and deployment, from data sourcing through to runtime integrations.
controls.discussion: Organizations should implement rigorous vetting procedures for all third-party components, dependencies, and data sources used in LLM systems. Establish secure data provenance tracking for training datasets and use cryptographic verification for model integrity and authenticity. Maintain detailed inventories of all dependencies and their versions, conduct regular security audits, and monitor for known vulnerabilities in plugins and integrated tools. Require signed commits and verified sources for critical components, and establish secure update and patching procedures.

controls.title: Data and Model Poisoning
controls.description: Data and Model Poisoning occurs when malicious actors inject contaminated information into training datasets or directly compromise model parameters, undermining the integrity of the LLM. This attack can introduce biased, false, or harmful content that becomes embedded in the model's outputs and decision-making processes. Attack vectors include injection of false information into training corpora, manipulation of fine-tuning datasets, direct alteration of model weights, and compromise of data sources used for continuous model updates.
controls.discussion: Implement rigorous vetting of all training data sources, ensuring authenticity and reliability before incorporation into model development pipelines. Restrict who can contribute to or modify training datasets and model parameters through role-based access management, and employ automated scanning tools to detect anomalies, statistical outliers, and suspicious patterns within training datasets. Establish continuous model testing to identify unexpected behavioral shifts or performance degradation post-deployment. Maintain detailed version control and audit trails of all modifications to datasets and models.

controls.title: Improper Output Handling
controls.description: Improper Output Handling refers to insufficient validation, sanitization, and handling of the outputs generated by large language models before they are passed downstream to other components and systems. Since LLM-generated content can be controlled by prompt input, this behavior is similar to providing users indirect access to additional functionality. Successful exploitation can result in XSS, CSRF, SSRF, privilege escalation, or remote code execution on backend systems.
controls.discussion: Treat the model as any other untrusted user, adopting a zero-trust approach and applying proper input validation on all responses coming from the model to backend functions. Follow the OWASP ASVS guidelines for effective input validation and sanitization, and encode model output back to users to mitigate undesired code execution. Implement context-aware output encoding based on where the LLM output will be used, use parameterized queries for all database operations involving LLM output, and employ strict Content Security Policies to mitigate XSS risk from LLM-generated content.

controls.title: Excessive Agency
controls.description: Excessive Agency occurs when large language models are granted overly broad permissions or autonomous capabilities to take actions without sufficient human oversight, validation, or control mechanisms. This risk involves systems that can perform high-impact operations — such as accessing external systems, modifying data, transferring funds, or executing commands — with minimal human intervention. The vulnerability becomes particularly dangerous when the model misunderstands intent, experiences prompt injection attacks, or has been compromised through jailbreak techniques.
controls.discussion: Implement human-in-the-loop controls requiring explicit human approval before the system executes high-impact actions, with clear visibility into intended operations. Apply the principle of least privilege, granting LLM systems only the minimal permissions necessary for their intended purpose and avoiding broad administrative access. Require critical operations to be confirmed through separate authentication or authorization channels. Maintain comprehensive audit logs, implement rate limiting and throttling, and separate read-only operations from write/delete operations behind additional security boundaries.

controls.title: System Prompt Leakage
controls.description: System Prompt Leakage occurs when LLM applications fail to properly protect their underlying system prompts and instructions from being extracted by users or attackers. Through various prompt injection and elicitation techniques, attackers can reveal sensitive operational directives, training data hints, and internal system configurations. When system prompts are exposed, attackers gain insight into the model's constraints, capabilities, and intended behavior patterns, which can then be exploited to manipulate the system or bypass security controls.
controls.discussion: Implement strict input validation and sanitization controls to prevent injection attacks that could expose system prompts. Architecturally separate system prompts from user-controllable content through clear delimiters and design patterns. Monitor and sanitize LLM outputs to prevent accidental disclosure of system instructions, and restrict who can view or modify system prompts to development teams only. Design systems with the principle of least privilege, using minimal necessary instructions and avoiding embedding sensitive information such as API keys or credentials in prompts.

controls.title: Vector and Embedding Weaknesses
controls.description: Vector and Embedding Weaknesses present significant security risks in systems utilizing Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) with large language models. Weaknesses in how vectors and embeddings are generated, stored, or retrieved can be exploited to inject harmful content, manipulate model outputs, or access sensitive information. Risks include unauthorized access and data leakage from embeddings, cross-context information leaks in multi-tenant environments, embedding inversion attacks that recover source information, and data poisoning of the knowledge base.
controls.discussion: Implement fine-grained access controls and permission-aware vector and embedding stores, ensuring strict logical and access partitioning of datasets in the vector database to prevent unauthorized access between different user classes. Establish robust data validation pipelines for knowledge sources, regularly auditing and validating the integrity of the knowledge base for hidden codes and data poisoning. When combining data from different sources, thoroughly review the combined dataset and tag and classify data to control access levels. Maintain detailed immutable logs of retrieval activities to detect and respond promptly to suspicious behavior.

controls.title: Misinformation
controls.description: Misinformation occurs when a large language model generates false, misleading, or fabricated information (hallucinations) that appears credible, leading users to rely on inaccurate outputs. LLMs can produce content that is factually incorrect, contextually inappropriate, or entirely fabricated while presenting it with high confidence. This risk is amplified when LLM outputs are used for decision-making in high-stakes domains without adequate verification mechanisms, potentially leading to reputational harm, legal liability, and safety incidents.
controls.discussion: Establish fact-checking mechanisms and cross-reference LLM outputs against authoritative sources before presenting information to end users. Require LLMs to cite sources and maintain traceability for generated claims, enabling users to verify information independently. Design systems where LLM outputs inform rather than replace human decision-making, especially in domains requiring high accuracy and accountability. Educate users about LLM limitations and the importance of critical evaluation of AI-generated content, and conduct periodic testing of outputs for factual accuracy.

controls.title: Unbounded Consumption
controls.description: Unbounded Consumption encompasses the unrestricted use of LLM resources without proper safeguards, leading to potential abuse, denial of service attacks, or unexpectedly high operational costs. When applications lack adequate controls on API calls, token usage, or computational resources, malicious actors or unintended usage patterns can exhaust system capacity and financial budgets. This vulnerability arises from insufficient rate limiting, absent resource quotas, or inadequate monitoring mechanisms.
controls.discussion: Apply strict rate limits on API requests and token consumption per user, session, or application tier to prevent excessive usage. Establish hard limits on computational resources, concurrent requests, and monthly token budgets aligned with business requirements, and configure spending caps with automated notifications when approaching thresholds. Deploy continuous monitoring and alerting systems to detect anomalous consumption spikes or unusual behavioral patterns. Enforce authentication and authorization mechanisms to ensure only legitimate users access LLM resources, and validate input size and complexity to prevent resource-intensive requests.
